🟡 Regenerate Cascadia

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Regenerate Cascadia implements a carefully structured approach that flows from bioregion to watershed to ecoregion to local landscape, integrating indigenous knowledge and stewardship practices across the Cascadia bioregion.

Bioregional Context

Cascadia spans from Southeast Alaska to Cape Mendocino in California, extending east to the Yellowstone Caldera. Defined by the watersheds of the Fraser and Columbia Rivers, it includes 15 major watersheds and 75 distinct ecoregions, with 80% of its 18 million population living on just 12% of the land area.

Place
Cascadia, OR, United States
links
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nowoYmBRijw|https://youtu.be/Vgt9g24gi7Q|https://youtu.be/6w_kCz9oZ_8|https://regeneratecascadia.org/

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 🏔️ Geographic_and_Ecological_Mapping 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🎒 Learning_Programs 🖼️ Cultural_Hub_or_Learning_Center 🔀 Grants_Programs 🎉 Congressing_and_Events 🔘 Governance_Bodies ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles

Background


The Cascadia bioregion stretches roughly 2,500 miles from Southeast Alaska to Cape Mendocino, California, reaching east to the Yellowstone Caldera. The Fraser and Columbia watersheds frame 15 major watersheds and 75 ecoregions. The Cascadia subduction zone has shaped this landscape for millennia—home to more than a thousand generations who have lived bioregionally, with Indigenous peoples stewarding the land since time immemorial.

Today 18 million people live here; 80% occupy about 12% of the land, mostly along the Seattle–Portland–Vancouver corridor. That concentration forms a “thin green line” with outsized influence—between interior extractive economies and markets across the Pacific.

Origins and Network


Brandon Letsinger’s path with Cascadia began in 2005. He founded the Department of Bioregion, a Seattle-based 501(c)(3) with a mission of “a world of interconnected bioregions and bioregional movements.”

At the first Salmon Nation Edge Prize in 2023, he met Claire Atwell—community artist, weaver, and organizer in Victoria, British Columbia. Together they asked how to regenerate a whole watershed, ecoregion, and bioregion across jurisdictional lines.

Regenerate Cascadia runs as a program inside the Department of Bioregion and is growing a charitable society in British Columbia to support cross-border organizing.

Their guiding question is practical and sharp: If $100 million reached their landscapes tomorrow, would they be ready to receive it? Would it reach the communities and ecosystems doing regenerative work, on their own terms? So far, the answer has largely been no.

They work through nested frames—bioregion → watershed → ecoregion → landscape. Brandon describes watersheds and ecoregions as rooms in a house: the bioregion is the house; ecoregions are not the whole system alone. Landscapes within watersheds are where people step outside and connect. The aim is to link action across scales so people see work happening, learn from neighbors, and build coherence within and between places.

Activities

🔸

Regional Activation Tour

 🔸

Hubs and Guilds Development Program

 🔸

Cascadia Bioregion Program

 🔸

Cascadia Regeneration Fund

Vision and Legacy


They are forming an investor design circle and a funder community of practice from the recent Bioregion Conference and preparing collaborative financing forums for mid-2026. The aim is federated grant processes—coalitions around shared landscape visions—rather than isolated, project-by-project competition.

Regenerate Cascadia’s story is still unfolding: nested mapping from bioregion to doorstep, hubs and guilds that hold knowledge and money closer to place, and a regeneration fund meant to behave like the watersheds it serves. The open question remains whether Cascadia can organize quickly enough—in governance, relationships, and finance—for major investment to land where ecological and community work actually happens.
